ipa: ngang ˧ · huyền ˧˩ · sắc ˧˥ · hỏi ˧˩˧ · ngã ˧ˀ˥ · nặng ˧˩ˀ
description: Vietnamese pitch melody changes meaning, and the tone is written right into the word via its diacritic. Northern Hanoi speech has six. Picture a five-line staff, low (1) to high (5): the same syllable 'ma' becomes six different words depending on its contour. Two of them (ngã, nặng) also involve a glottal catch — your voice momentarily stops — which English never uses lexically. Learn to hear all six here; every Vietnamese word is one of them.

minimalPairs.b: ghost — a flat mid note, held level
but / which — start mid and drift down low, relaxed and a touch breathy
cheek / mother — push up from mid to high, bright and clear
grave / tomb — dip down then curl back up, a smooth question-like scoop
horse / code — rise like sắc but with a glottal CATCH in the middle, as if your voice cracks then finishes higher
rice seedling — drop low, short and hard, cut off by a glottal stop, like a grunted 'uh!'

description: The unmarked tone: a steady mid-pitch note held flat, maybe a touch higher than your rest pitch. No rise, no fall, no catch. The neutral baseline the other five bend away from.
Start around mid and slope gently down to the bottom of your range, relaxed and slightly breathy — like a soft, tired exhale. Lower and calmer than English falling intonation.

minimalPairs.b: to return / go home — low falling

description: Climb from mid up to the top of your range, bright and clean, no catch. The melody of an eager English 'yeah?' going up. Its evil twin is ngã — same rise, but ngã adds a glottal break.

minimalPairs.b: water / country — rising on a short, stopped syllable

description: Begin mid, dip down, then curl back up — a smooth valley, no glottal catch (in the North). It sounds like a drawn-out, thoughtful English 'really…?'. Distinct from ngã, which breaks in the middle; hỏi is smooth throughout.

minimalPairs.b: to ask — the tone named after itself: dip and rise

ipa: ˧ˀ˥ (3ʔ5 — rise with a mid glottal stop)
description: The hardest tone for English speakers. Start mid, rise toward high like sắc — but crack your voice with a glottal stop partway through, then finish the rise. It's a rising note interrupted by a tiny 'uh-oh' catch. If you can't do the break yet, Northern speakers will still understand a clean sắc, but aim for the catch.

minimalPairs.b: horse / code (ngã) — vs má 'cheek' (sắc): same rise, ngã has the break
also / too — very common; rise with a glottal catch

description: A low, short, heavy tone that drops fast and is cut off hard by a glottal stop — the syllable feels clipped and 'weighted', like a grunted 'uh!' with the throat snapping shut. Shorter than all the others.

minimalPairs.b: rice seedling — heavy, cut off short
one — the number, on a short stopped syllable

description: Two letters that trip up beginners. đ (d with a bar) is an implosive 'd' — a normal-sounding d made by pulling air slightly inward, as in đi 'to go'. Plain d and the digraph gi are NOT 'd' at all in the North — they're a 'z' sound: da 'skin' = 'za', gia đình 'family' = 'za-din'.

In Northern speech, three spellings collapse to one sound, /z/: d (da 'zah'), gi (giá 'záh'), and r (rồi 'zồi' 'already'). In the South these stay distinct (r is a real 'r'). Learn the /z/ for Hanoi, but recognize the spellings are different words.

ơ is a mid central unrounded vowel, like the 'u' in 'fur' with unrounded lips, held long: cơm 'rice', bơ 'butter/avocado'. â is the same quality but SHORT: sân 'yard'. Don't round the lips — that turns it into ô ('oh').

minimalPairs.b: short â — vs côn/cơn different vowels

description: Like the 'oo' in 'food' but with lips pulled back flat and unrounded — the same alien vowel Thai has. Found in tư 'four/private', chưa 'not yet', nước 'water'. Smile while you say 'oo'.

After rounded back vowels (o, ô, u), a final -ng or -c is made with a simultaneous lip closure — học 'to study' ends with the lips popping shut ('hawkp'), and không 'no' ends with rounded lips ('khawngm'). English speakers miss the lip-round on the final.

minimalPairs.b: to study — final -c with lips closing

description: nh is a palatal 'ny' (like Spanish ñ or 'canyon'): nhà 'house' = 'nyah'. As a final it colours the vowel toward an 'i/ng' glide: anh 'older brother' ≈ 'ang/ain'.
Vietnamese starts words with the 'ng' sound English only puts at the end: ngon 'delicious', người 'person', nghe 'to listen' (ngh before e/i/ê). Say 'singer', hold the 'ng', drop the 'si—'.
kh is not an English 'k' — it's a raspy velar fricative, the 'ch' in 'loch' or German 'Bach': không 'no' = 'khong' with friction at the back of the throat, khách 'guest'.

minimalPairs.b: no — kh, not a clean k

description: In the North, ch and tr are both a soft unaspirated 'ch': trà 'tea' and cha 'father' start alike ('cha'). In the South tr is a distinct retroflex. Learn the Hanoi merger; note the spellings differ.
Like Thai, final c/t/p are cut off with no release — một 'one' ends with the tongue at 't', air stopped, no 'tuh'. These short stopped syllables only ever carry sắc or nặng (rising or heavy).
Three centering glides that end in a soft 'ə': tiền 'money' (iê), muốn 'to want' (uô), người 'person' (ươ). Glide smoothly into the schwa — one syllable, and don't drop the tone.

grapheme: One syllable, one tone, written apart
description: Vietnamese is written with a space around every syllable, and each syllable carries exactly one tone at roughly equal length — no reduced/schwa syllables like English. 'Việt Nam' is two full tone-bearing beats, not 'vyet-num'. Give every syllable its note.

minimalPairs.b: two equal tone-bearing syllables (nặng, then level)
'thank you' — two beats: hỏi, then level
